questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Let $\alpha < \beta < \gamma$ be three numbers in G.P. Let $f(x)=x^3-a x^2+b x-8$ be a polynomial such that $f(x)=0$ has three roots $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$, where $\alpha, \gamma$ are integers.Question: Let $g(x)=f(x-\beta)$, then the roots of the equation $g(x)=0$ are in:
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=x^3-a x^2+b x-8 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \beta=2 \text { and } b=2 a \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\frac{2}{p} \text { and } \gamma=2 p \text { ( } p \text { is the common ratio) } \\
& \alpha \in I \Rightarrow p=-1,1,2,-2 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=-2,2,1,-1 ; \quad \beta=2,2,2,2 ; \quad \gamma=-2,2,4,-4 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=1, \beta=2, \gamma=4 \quad \text { and } a=7 ; \quad b=14
\end{aligned}
$$

Roots of the equation $f(x-\beta)=0$ are $\alpha+\beta, 2 \beta, \gamma+\beta$ which are in H.P.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Let $\alpha < \beta < \gamma$ be three numbers in G.P. Let $f(x)=x^3-a x^2+b x-8$ be a polynomial such that $f(x)=0$ has three roots $\alpha, \beta$ and $\gamma$, where $\alpha, \gamma$ are integers.Question: Let $y=g(x)$ be a twice differentiable function such that $g(\alpha)=0, g(\beta)=2, g(\gamma)=-3$, $g(a)=5, g(b)=0$, then number of minimum distinct real roots of the equation $\left(g^{\prime}(x)\right)^2+g(x) \cdot g^{\prime \prime}(x)=0$ in $[1,14]$ is:
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=x^3-a x^2+b x-8 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \beta=2 \text { and } b=2 a \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=\frac{2}{p} \text { and } \gamma=2 p \text { ( } p \text { is the common ratio) } \\
& \alpha \in I \Rightarrow p=-1,1,2,-2 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=-2,2,1,-1 ; \quad \beta=2,2,2,2 ; \quad \gamma=-2,2,4,-4 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \alpha=1, \beta=2, \gamma=4 \quad \text { and } a=7 ; \quad b=14
\end{aligned}
$$

Roots of the equation $f(x-\beta)=0$ are $\alpha+\beta, 2 \beta, \gamma+\beta$ which are in H.P.
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: The sum of an infinitely decreasing geometric progression whose first term is a and common ratio $r$, is equal to least value of the quadratic trinomial $P(x)=3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}$, in $[0,2]$. Also the first term of the geometric progression is equal to the square of its common ratioQuestion: The value of $\left(r^2+2 r\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $P(x)=3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}=3\left[x^2-\frac{1}{3} x+\frac{25}{36}\right]{ }^3\left(\left(x-\frac{1}{6}\right)^2+\frac{2}{3}\right)$ $\left.\Rightarrow P(x)\right|_{\text {min. }}=2$, occurs at $x=\frac{1}{6}$. Given, $\frac{a}{1-r}=2$ $\qquad......(1)$ and $\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{r}^2$ $\qquad......(2)$ $\therefore$ Solving (1) and (2), we get $\begin{aligned} & \frac{r^2}{1-r}=2 \Rightarrow r^2=2-2 r \Rightarrow r^2+2 r=2 \\ \therefore & r=(\sqrt{3}-1) \qquad \qquad [\text { As } r \in(0,1)]\\ \Rightarrow & a=r^2=4-2 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$ Let $f(x)=x^2+(k+1) x+a+2 \sqrt{3}$ Now, $f(x)=0 \Rightarrow a^2+(k+1) x+4=0$. Since 2 lies between the roots, hence $f(2) \lt 0$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 4+2 k+2+4 \lt 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 k+10 \lt 0 \Rightarrow k \lt -5 \end{aligned}$ So, $\mathrm{k}_{\text {max }}=-6$ Let $y=\frac{P(x)}{P^{\prime}(x)}=\frac{3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}}{6 x-1} \Rightarrow 3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}=6 x y-y$ $\Rightarrow 3 x^2-x(1+6 y)+\frac{25}{12}+y=0$ As, $x \in R$ so $D \geq 0$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(6 y+1)^2-12\left(\frac{25+12 y}{12}\right) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 36 y^2+12 y+1-25-12 y \geq 0 \Rightarrow 36 y^2-24 \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 y^2-2 \geq 0 \end{aligned}$ Range of $\mathrm{y}=\left(-\infty,-\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\right] \cup\left[\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}, \infty\right)=(-\infty, \mathrm{p}] \cup[\mathrm{q}, \infty)$
So, $\mathrm{p}=-\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ and $\mathrm{q}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ Hence, $(p+q)=0$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.q: Paragraph: The sum of an infinitely decreasing geometric progression whose first term is a and common ratio $r$, is equal to least value of the quadratic trinomial $P(x)=3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}$, in $[0,2]$. Also the first term of the geometric progression is equal to the square of its common ratioQuestion: If the minimum value of $P(x)$ lies between roots of equation $x^2+(k+1) x+a+2 \sqrt{3}=0$, then the maximum integral value of $k$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $P(x)=3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}=3\left[x^2-\frac{1}{3} x+\frac{25}{36}\right]{ }^3\left(\left(x-\frac{1}{6}\right)^2+\frac{2}{3}\right)$ $\left.\Rightarrow P(x)\right|_{\text {min. }}=2$, occurs at $x=\frac{1}{6}$. Given, $\frac{a}{1-r}=2$ $\qquad......(1)$ and $\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{r}^2$ $\qquad......(2)$ $\therefore$ Solving (1) and (2), we get $\begin{aligned} & \frac{r^2}{1-r}=2 \Rightarrow r^2=2-2 r \Rightarrow r^2+2 r=2 \\ \therefore & r=(\sqrt{3}-1) \qquad \qquad [\text { As } r \in(0,1)]\\ \Rightarrow & a=r^2=4-2 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$ Let $f(x)=x^2+(k+1) x+a+2 \sqrt{3}$ Now, $f(x)=0 \Rightarrow a^2+(k+1) x+4=0$. Since 2 lies between the roots, hence $f(2) \lt 0$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 4+2 k+2+4 \lt 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 k+10 \lt 0 \Rightarrow k \lt -5 \end{aligned}$ So, $\mathrm{k}_{\text {max }}=-6$ Let $y=\frac{P(x)}{P^{\prime}(x)}=\frac{3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}}{6 x-1} \Rightarrow 3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}=6 x y-y$ $\Rightarrow 3 x^2-x(1+6 y)+\frac{25}{12}+y=0$ As, $x \in R$ so $D \geq 0$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(6 y+1)^2-12\left(\frac{25+12 y}{12}\right) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 36 y^2+12 y+1-25-12 y \geq 0 \Rightarrow 36 y^2-24 \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 y^2-2 \geq 0 \end{aligned}$ Range of $\mathrm{y}=\left(-\infty,-\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\right] \cup\left[\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}, \infty\right)=(-\infty, \mathrm{p}] \cup[\mathrm{q}, \infty)$
So, $\mathrm{p}=-\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ and $\mathrm{q}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ Hence, $(p+q)=0$

questions.q: Paragraph: The sum of an infinitely decreasing geometric progression whose first term is a and common ratio $r$, is equal to least value of the quadratic trinomial $P(x)=3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}$, in $[0,2]$. Also the first term of the geometric progression is equal to the square of its common ratioQuestion: If the range of function $y=\frac{P(x)}{P^{\prime}(x)}$ is $(-\infty, p] \cup[q, \infty)$, then $(p+q)$, is
questions.solution:
Given, $P(x)=3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}=3\left[x^2-\frac{1}{3} x+\frac{25}{36}\right]{ }^3\left(\left(x-\frac{1}{6}\right)^2+\frac{2}{3}\right)$ $\left.\Rightarrow P(x)\right|_{\text {min. }}=2$, occurs at $x=\frac{1}{6}$. Given, $\frac{a}{1-r}=2$ $\qquad......(1)$ and $\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{r}^2$ $\qquad......(2)$ $\therefore$ Solving (1) and (2), we get $\begin{aligned} & \frac{r^2}{1-r}=2 \Rightarrow r^2=2-2 r \Rightarrow r^2+2 r=2 \\ \therefore & r=(\sqrt{3}-1) \qquad \qquad [\text { As } r \in(0,1)]\\ \Rightarrow & a=r^2=4-2 \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$ Let $f(x)=x^2+(k+1) x+a+2 \sqrt{3}$ Now, $f(x)=0 \Rightarrow a^2+(k+1) x+4=0$. Since 2 lies between the roots, hence $f(2) \lt 0$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 4+2 k+2+4 \lt 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 k+10 \lt 0 \Rightarrow k \lt -5 \end{aligned}$ So, $\mathrm{k}_{\text {max }}=-6$ Let $y=\frac{P(x)}{P^{\prime}(x)}=\frac{3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}}{6 x-1} \Rightarrow 3 x^2-x+\frac{25}{12}=6 x y-y$ $\Rightarrow 3 x^2-x(1+6 y)+\frac{25}{12}+y=0$ As, $x \in R$ so $D \geq 0$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(6 y+1)^2-12\left(\frac{25+12 y}{12}\right) \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 36 y^2+12 y+1-25-12 y \geq 0 \Rightarrow 36 y^2-24 \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 y^2-2 \geq 0 \end{aligned}$ Range of $\mathrm{y}=\left(-\infty,-\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\right] \cup\left[\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}, \infty\right)=(-\infty, \mathrm{p}] \cup[\mathrm{q}, \infty)$
So, $\mathrm{p}=-\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ and $\mathrm{q}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ Hence, $(p+q)=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Let $ \lt a_n\gt$ and $ \lt b_n\gt$ be the arithmetic sequences each with common difference 2 such that $a_1 \lt b_1$ and let $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{k}}, \mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{b}_{\mathrm{k}}$. Suppose that the points $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}\right), \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\mathrm{b}_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{n}}\right)$ are all lying on the parabola $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{px}{ }^2+$ $q x+r$ where $p, q, r$ are constants.Question: The value of $p$ equals
questions.solution:
(i) Given $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{a}_1+\mathrm{a}_2+\mathrm{a}_3+\ldots \ldots .+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}$ where $a_1, a_2, \ldots \ldots . ., a_n$ are in A.P. with $d=2$ and $d_n=b_1+b_2+b_3+\ldots \ldots .+b_n$ where $b_1, b_2, b_3, \ldots \ldots, b_n$ are in A.P. with $d=2$ Also, $\left(a_n, c_n\right)$ lies on $y=p x^2+q x+r$
Now, $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{pa}_{\mathrm{n}}^2+\mathrm{qa}_{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{r}$ .....(1)
$c_{n-1}=p a_{n-1}^2+q a_{n-1}+r$ .....(2)
$\therefore$ From (1) and (2), we get
$c_n-c_{n-1}=p\left(a_n^2-a_{n-1}^2\right)+q\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow a_n=p\left(a_n+a_{n-1}\right)\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)+q\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)$
$a_n=\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)\left[p\left(a_n+a_{n-1}\right)+q\right]$.....(3) $\left(a_n-a_{n-1}=d\right)$
On putting $n=2$ and 3 in equation (3), we get
$a_2=d\left[p\left(a_2+a_1\right)+q\right]$ .....(4)
$a_3=d\left[p\left(a_3+a_2\right)+q\right]$.....(5)
Now (5) - (4), we get
$\underbrace{\mathrm{a}_3-\mathrm{a}_2}_{=\mathrm{d}}=\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{p} \underbrace{\left(\mathrm{a}_3-\mathrm{a}_1\right)}_{=2 \mathrm{~d}}]$
$4 p=1 \Rightarrow p=\frac{1}{4}$
(ii) To find q :
$\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{pa}_{\mathrm{n}}^2+\mathrm{qa} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{r}$

On putting $\mathrm{n}=1,2$ in above equation, we get
$\mathrm{c}_1=\mathrm{a}_1=\mathrm{pa}_1^2+\mathrm{qa}_1+\mathrm{r}$.....(1)
$\mathrm{c}_2=\mathrm{a}_1+\mathrm{a}_2=\mathrm{pa}_2^2+\mathrm{qa}_2+\mathrm{r}$.....(2)
$\begin{aligned} & \text { but } a_2=a_1+2 \\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2 a_1+2=p\left(a_1+2\right)^2+q\left(a_1+2\right)+r \\ & \quad\left(p a_1^2+q a_1+r\right)+4 a_1 p+4 p+2 q(4 p=1) \\ & 2 a_1+2=c_1+a_1+1+2 q\left(\because c_1=a_1\right) \\ & 2 a_1+2=2 a_1+1+2 q \Rightarrow q=\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (iii) if } r=0 \text {, then } \\ & c_1=p a_1^2+q a_1 \\ & a_1=\frac{1}{4} a_1^2+\frac{1}{2} a_1\left(\because c_1=a_1\right) \\ & a_1^2-2 a_1=0 \Rightarrow a_1=0 \text { or } a_1=2 \\ & \text { Also } d_1=\frac{1}{4} b_1^2+q b_1\left(\because q=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } d_1=b_1\right) \\ & b_1=\frac{1}{4} b_1^2+\frac{1}{2} b_1 \\ & b_1^2-2 b_1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow b_1=0 \text { or } b_1=2 \\ & \text { But } a_1 \lt b_1 \\ & \Rightarrow a_1=0 \text { and } b_1=2\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Let $ \lt a_n\gt$ and $ \lt b_n\gt$ be the arithmetic sequences each with common difference 2 such that $a_1 \lt b_1$ and let $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{k}}, \mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{b}_{\mathrm{k}}$. Suppose that the points $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}\right), \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\mathrm{b}_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{n}}\right)$ are all lying on the parabola $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{px}{ }^2+$ $q x+r$ where $p, q, r$ are constants.Question: The value of $q$ equals
questions.solution:
(i) Given $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{a}_1+\mathrm{a}_2+\mathrm{a}_3+\ldots \ldots .+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}$ where $a_1, a_2, \ldots \ldots . ., a_n$ are in A.P. with $d=2$ and $d_n=b_1+b_2+b_3+\ldots \ldots .+b_n$ where $b_1, b_2, b_3, \ldots \ldots, b_n$ are in A.P. with $d=2$ Also, $\left(a_n, c_n\right)$ lies on $y=p x^2+q x+r$
Now, $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{pa}_{\mathrm{n}}^2+\mathrm{qa}_{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{r}$ .....(1)
$c_{n-1}=p a_{n-1}^2+q a_{n-1}+r$ .....(2)
$\therefore$ From (1) and (2), we get
$c_n-c_{n-1}=p\left(a_n^2-a_{n-1}^2\right)+q\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow a_n=p\left(a_n+a_{n-1}\right)\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)+q\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)$
$a_n=\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)\left[p\left(a_n+a_{n-1}\right)+q\right]$.....(3) $\left(a_n-a_{n-1}=d\right)$
On putting $n=2$ and 3 in equation (3), we get
$a_2=d\left[p\left(a_2+a_1\right)+q\right]$ .....(4)
$a_3=d\left[p\left(a_3+a_2\right)+q\right]$.....(5)
Now (5) - (4), we get
$\underbrace{\mathrm{a}_3-\mathrm{a}_2}_{=\mathrm{d}}=\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{p} \underbrace{\left(\mathrm{a}_3-\mathrm{a}_1\right)}_{=2 \mathrm{~d}}]$
$4 p=1 \Rightarrow p=\frac{1}{4}$
(ii) To find q :
$\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{pa}_{\mathrm{n}}^2+\mathrm{qa} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{r}$

On putting $\mathrm{n}=1,2$ in above equation, we get
$\mathrm{c}_1=\mathrm{a}_1=\mathrm{pa}_1^2+\mathrm{qa}_1+\mathrm{r}$.....(1)
$\mathrm{c}_2=\mathrm{a}_1+\mathrm{a}_2=\mathrm{pa}_2^2+\mathrm{qa}_2+\mathrm{r}$.....(2)
$\begin{aligned} & \text { but } a_2=a_1+2 \\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2 a_1+2=p\left(a_1+2\right)^2+q\left(a_1+2\right)+r \\ & \quad\left(p a_1^2+q a_1+r\right)+4 a_1 p+4 p+2 q(4 p=1) \\ & 2 a_1+2=c_1+a_1+1+2 q\left(\because c_1=a_1\right) \\ & 2 a_1+2=2 a_1+1+2 q \Rightarrow q=\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (iii) if } r=0 \text {, then } \\ & c_1=p a_1^2+q a_1 \\ & a_1=\frac{1}{4} a_1^2+\frac{1}{2} a_1\left(\because c_1=a_1\right) \\ & a_1^2-2 a_1=0 \Rightarrow a_1=0 \text { or } a_1=2 \\ & \text { Also } d_1=\frac{1}{4} b_1^2+q b_1\left(\because q=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } d_1=b_1\right) \\ & b_1=\frac{1}{4} b_1^2+\frac{1}{2} b_1 \\ & b_1^2-2 b_1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow b_1=0 \text { or } b_1=2 \\ & \text { But } a_1 \lt b_1 \\ & \Rightarrow a_1=0 \text { and } b_1=2\end{aligned}$
questions.source: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Most Important Selected Qs for JEE Advanced
questions.q: Paragraph: Let $ \lt a_n\gt$ and $ \lt b_n\gt$ be the arithmetic sequences each with common difference 2 such that $a_1 \lt b_1$ and let $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{k}}, \mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{n}}=\sum_{\mathrm{k}=1}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{b}_{\mathrm{k}}$. Suppose that the points $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}\right), \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\mathrm{b}_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{n}}\right)$ are all lying on the parabola $\mathrm{C}: \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{px}{ }^2+$ $q x+r$ where $p, q, r$ are constants.Question: If $r=0$ then the value of $a_1$ and $b_1$ are
questions.solution:
(i) Given $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{a}_1+\mathrm{a}_2+\mathrm{a}_3+\ldots \ldots .+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}$ where $a_1, a_2, \ldots \ldots . ., a_n$ are in A.P. with $d=2$ and $d_n=b_1+b_2+b_3+\ldots \ldots .+b_n$ where $b_1, b_2, b_3, \ldots \ldots, b_n$ are in A.P. with $d=2$ Also, $\left(a_n, c_n\right)$ lies on $y=p x^2+q x+r$
Now, $\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{pa}_{\mathrm{n}}^2+\mathrm{qa}_{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{r}$ .....(1)
$c_{n-1}=p a_{n-1}^2+q a_{n-1}+r$ .....(2)
$\therefore$ From (1) and (2), we get
$c_n-c_{n-1}=p\left(a_n^2-a_{n-1}^2\right)+q\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow a_n=p\left(a_n+a_{n-1}\right)\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)+q\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)$
$a_n=\left(a_n-a_{n-1}\right)\left[p\left(a_n+a_{n-1}\right)+q\right]$.....(3) $\left(a_n-a_{n-1}=d\right)$
On putting $n=2$ and 3 in equation (3), we get
$a_2=d\left[p\left(a_2+a_1\right)+q\right]$ .....(4)
$a_3=d\left[p\left(a_3+a_2\right)+q\right]$.....(5)
Now (5) - (4), we get
$\underbrace{\mathrm{a}_3-\mathrm{a}_2}_{=\mathrm{d}}=\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{p} \underbrace{\left(\mathrm{a}_3-\mathrm{a}_1\right)}_{=2 \mathrm{~d}}]$
$4 p=1 \Rightarrow p=\frac{1}{4}$
(ii) To find q :
$\mathrm{c}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{pa}_{\mathrm{n}}^2+\mathrm{qa} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}+\mathrm{r}$

On putting $\mathrm{n}=1,2$ in above equation, we get
$\mathrm{c}_1=\mathrm{a}_1=\mathrm{pa}_1^2+\mathrm{qa}_1+\mathrm{r}$.....(1)
$\mathrm{c}_2=\mathrm{a}_1+\mathrm{a}_2=\mathrm{pa}_2^2+\mathrm{qa}_2+\mathrm{r}$.....(2)
$\begin{aligned} & \text { but } a_2=a_1+2 \\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 2 a_1+2=p\left(a_1+2\right)^2+q\left(a_1+2\right)+r \\ & \quad\left(p a_1^2+q a_1+r\right)+4 a_1 p+4 p+2 q(4 p=1) \\ & 2 a_1+2=c_1+a_1+1+2 q\left(\because c_1=a_1\right) \\ & 2 a_1+2=2 a_1+1+2 q \Rightarrow q=\frac{1}{2}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (iii) if } r=0 \text {, then } \\ & c_1=p a_1^2+q a_1 \\ & a_1=\frac{1}{4} a_1^2+\frac{1}{2} a_1\left(\because c_1=a_1\right) \\ & a_1^2-2 a_1=0 \Rightarrow a_1=0 \text { or } a_1=2 \\ & \text { Also } d_1=\frac{1}{4} b_1^2+q b_1\left(\because q=\frac{1}{2} \text { and } d_1=b_1\right) \\ & b_1=\frac{1}{4} b_1^2+\frac{1}{2} b_1 \\ & b_1^2-2 b_1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow b_1=0 \text { or } b_1=2 \\ & \text { But } a_1 \lt b_1 \\ & \Rightarrow a_1=0 \text { and } b_1=2\end{aligned}$
